stimuli.description: Stop signal star (#jspsych-stop-signal-SS) overlaid on a shape or probe-letter trial; participant must withhold response.
Circle shape stimulus shown during the simple-stop-signal block (go trial). Response key (',' or '.') depends on counterbalanced group_index.

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img[src*="circle"]'); if (!img) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const k = g % 4; return (k === 0 || k === 1) ? ',' : '.'; })()

stimuli.description: Square shape stimulus shown during the simple-stop-signal block (go trial). Response key (',' or '.') depends on counterbalanced group_index.

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img[src*="square"]'); if (!img) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const k = g % 4; return (k === 0 || k === 1) ? '.' : ','; })()

stimuli.description: Letter probe trial (memory-only practice or integrated phase). The single probe letter IS contained in the most recent memory-set (window.lastShownLetters). Wrapper differs by phase: '#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus .probe-letter' for integrated, '#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .letter-text' for memory-only practice.

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus .probe-letter') || document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .letter-text'); if (!el) return false; const letter = (el.textContent || '').trim(); const shown = window.lastShownLetters || ''; return shown.length > 0 && letter.length === 1 && shown.indexOf(letter) >= 0; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const k = g % 4; return (k === 0 || k === 2) ? 'x' : 'z'; })()

stimuli.description: Letter probe trial (memory-only practice or integrated phase). The single probe letter is NOT in the most recent memory-set (window.lastShownLetters).

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus .probe-letter') || document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .letter-text'); if (!el) return false; const letter = (el.textContent || '').trim(); const shown = window.lastShownLetters || ''; return shown.length > 0 && letter.length === 1 && shown.indexOf(letter) < 0; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const k = g % 4; return (k === 0 || k === 2) ? 'z' : 'x'; })()

phase_detection.test: (() => { try { const last = jsPsych.data.get().last(1).values()[0]; return !!(last && last.exp_stage === 'test'); } catch (e) { return false; } })()
phase_detection.practice: (() => { try { const last = jsPsych.data.get().last(1).values()[0]; return !!(last && last.exp_stage === 'practice'); } catch (e) { return false; } })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { if (document.getElementById('finished_message')) return false; if (document.getElementById('jspsych-instructions-next') || document.getElementById('jspsych-fullscreen-btn')) return false; const el = document.getElementById('jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!el) return false; const t = el.textContent || ''; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img[src*="circle"]') || document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img[src*="square"]')) return false; if (document.querySelector('.probe-letter') || document.querySelector('.letter-text')) return false; return /press.{0,5}enter/i.test(t); })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const t = el.textContent || ''; const m = t.match(/Press the (\w) key/i); if (m) return m[1].toLowerCase(); if (/third to last letter/i.test(t)) return 'x'; if (/second to last letter/i.test(t)) return 'y'; if (/last letter/i.test(t)) return 'z'; if (/first letter/i.test(t)) return 'a'; if (/second letter/i.test(t)) return 'b'; if (/third letter/i.test(t)) return 'c'; return null; })()

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (BEESTS fits to Verbruggen et al. 2008 healthy-control go-RT)
citations.quote: Posterior means for the go-RT ex-Gaussian parameters in the healthy control sample were mu ~ 390 ms, sigma ~ 50 ms, and tau ~ 90 ms.

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean go-RTs in two-choice stop-signal tasks typically fall in the 450-550 ms range; ex-Gaussian decompositions of the go distribution place mu near 380-420 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S.J.; Mewhort, D.J.K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.table_or_figure: Table 1 (ex-Gaussian fits to simple choice RT)
citations.quote: Mu estimates for simple two-choice tasks fell in the 340-410 ms range across subjects.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.quote: Go-RT sigma posterior means fell in the 40-65 ms range across the simulation and empirical samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S.J.; Mewhort, D.J.K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma values ranged from 35 to 70 ms across conditions and subjects.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.quote: Go-RT tau posterior means were in the 70-130 ms range, with sample-specific estimates near 90 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S.J.; Mewhort, D.J.K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau (exponential component) ranged from approximately 65 to 150 ms across simple-choice conditions.

shape_go.rationale: Simple two-choice shape discrimination (circle vs. square) in a stop-signal go context typically yields mean go-RTs ~450-500ms with ex-Gaussian fits in the mu=350-400, sigma=40-60, tau=80-120 ranges (Verbruggen & Logan 2008; Matzke et al. 2013 BEESTS fits).

citations.title: High-speed scanning in human memory
citations.quote: Positive (in-set) probe RT for set sizes 1-6 ranged from approximately 420 ms (set size 1) to 600 ms (set size 6); intercept ~390 ms.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (ex-Gaussian fits to Sternberg probe RT)
citations.quote: For positive probes at memory-set size 4, mu estimates were near 540-620 ms across subjects.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for in-set Sternberg probes ranged from 55 to 110 ms across set sizes and subjects.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Tau (exponential) component for positive probes increased with set size; estimates ranged from approximately 120 to 250 ms.

citations.title: A decision model for accuracy and response latency in recognition memory
citations.quote: The exponential tail of Sternberg-style probe RT distributions reflects search-time variability and consistently scales upward with set size.

in_memory_set.rationale: Sternberg-style memory-probe match trials are slower than simple choice RT due to working-memory search/comparison (~750-900ms mean). Probe RTs scale with set size; ex-Gaussian tail (tau ~150-200ms) reflects occasional slow searches (Sternberg 1969; Hockley 1984).

citations.title: High-speed scanning in human memory
citations.quote: Negative-probe (out-of-set) RTs averaged 20-50 ms slower than positive probes across set sizes; intercept and slope for negative probes ~430 ms intercept.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Negative-probe mu estimates were systematically 20-50 ms higher than positive-probe mu at matched set sizes (e.g., ~580-660 ms at set size 4).

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Sigma for negative probes was slightly larger than for positive probes, with estimates from 60 to 120 ms across set sizes.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Tau for negative probes consistently exceeded tau for positive probes; estimates ranged from approximately 140 to 280 ms, with the larger tail attributed to exhaustive-scan variability.

not_in_memory_set.rationale: Non-match (negative) probes in Sternberg-style tasks are reliably 20-50ms slower than match probes because exhaustive scanning is required to confirm absence (Sternberg 1966, 1969; Hockley 1984). Larger tau reflects greater variability of exhaustive comparison.

citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.table_or_figure: Figure 3 (failed-stop RT vs. go-RT under the independent race model)
citations.quote: Failed-stop (signal-respond) RTs must lie in the lower tail of the go-RT distribution; observed means are typically 50-100 ms faster than mean go-RT.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions are leftward-shifted relative to go-RT; recovered mu typically near 320-380 ms when go-mu is 380-420 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.quote: Signal-respond sigma estimates fell in the 30-55 ms range, slightly compressed relative to go-RT sigma due to truncation by the stop-signal race.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.quote: Signal-respond tau is substantially smaller than go-tau (the slow tail is removed by the stop process); typical estimates 40-110 ms.

stop.rationale: Failed-stop (commission-error) RTs are systematically faster than mean go-RTs per the independent race model — the go process won the race because it was unusually fast. Typical mean failed-stop RT runs ~50-100ms below mean go-RT (Logan & Cowan 1984; Verbruggen & Logan 2008). Capped further at runtime by the trial_interrupt.failure_rt_cap_fraction=0.3.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J.; Farrell, S.; Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f-noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations in single-subject RT series typically ranged from .10 to .30 across speeded-decision paradigms.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Short-lag autocorrelation coefficients in RT series cluster near 0.15-0.25 across cognitive tasks.

autocorrelation.rationale: Lag-1 RT autocorrelation in speeded-decision tasks consistently runs phi ~0.15-0.25 (Wagenmakers et al. 2004; Gilden 2001). General to all speeded-RT paradigms; not paradigm-specific.

citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Time-on-task produces a steady positive RT drift on the order of 5-15 ms per 100 trials in continuous speeded tasks.

citations.title: Mental fatigue: Costs and benefits
citations.quote: Fatigue-related RT slowing accumulates roughly linearly across blocks at a small per-trial rate (typically <0.15 ms/trial) in healthy young adults during ~60-90-minute sessions.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Slow positive RT drift across long sessions is well-documented in dual-task and stop-signal sessions (~5-10ms per 100 trials); reflects time-on-task fatigue distinct from short-term vigilance lapses (Lim & Dinges 2008).

condition_repetition.rationale: Shape-go trials in this design are not designed to elicit condition-repetition priming as a primary effect, and Sternberg probe in/not-in-memory transitions reflect new memory sets each trial, not stable condition repeats. No primary literature documents a robust condition-repetition effect for this integrated design.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f-style long-range noise is detectable in very long RT series but redundant with autocorrelation at the session lengths used here (~150-300 trials). Enabling both risks double-counting low-frequency RT structure.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: There is no published Sternberg-style or stop-signal literature documenting systematic lag-1 condition-pair RT modulation analogous to the conflict-task congruency-sequence effect for these condition transitions.

citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.table_or_figure: Table 1 (RT on N+1 trials after errors)
citations.quote: RTs on the trial following an error were on average 30-50 ms slower than RTs following correct responses.

citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing across paradigms reliably yields RT increases of approximately 20-60 ms on the immediately following trial.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go-trial RTs immediately following stop-signal trials were 60-110 ms slower than baseline go-RT, with post-successful-inhibit larger than post-failed-inhibit.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F.; Logan, G.D.; Liefooghe, B.; Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing on the subsequent go trial ranged from 60 to 120 ms across experiments and was attributed to between-trial control adjustments rather than priming.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is robust in stop-signal and Sternberg paradigms (~30-50ms; Rabbitt 1966; Danielmeier & Ullsperger 2011). Post-stop slowing (post-successful-inhibition) is reliably larger (~60-100ms) and is canonical for the stop-signal class (Verbruggen et al. 2008; Bissett & Logan 2011). The interrupt trigger captures stop-signal trials (regardless of stop success) per the executor's trial_interrupt.detection_condition='stop' wiring; exclusive_with_prior_triggers prevents double-counting when a failed-stop is also an error.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Initial-block RTs exceeded asymptotic RTs by 70-150 ms in multi-component speeded tasks.

citations.title: Bending the power law: A CMPL theory of strategy shifts and the automatization of cognitive skills
citations.quote: Across multiple cognitive-task experiments, the difference between block-1 mean RT and asymptotic mean RT fell in the range of approximately 50-150 ms.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: RT typically approached an asymptote within the first 3-5 practice blocks for simple speeded-decision tasks.

citations.title: Bending the power law
citations.quote: Visible practice gains were concentrated in the first 2-4 blocks of training across experiments.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS: Bayesian Estimation of Ex-Gaussian STop-Signal Reaction Time Distributions
citations.quote: Stop-signal experimental blocks typically contained 24-64 trials in published reference data.

citations.title: High-speed scanning in human memory
citations.quote: Probe trials were administered in blocks of approximately 24-48 trials within each set-size condition.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Power-law exponents for RT decay across practice typically ranged from 0.2 to 0.5 across task types.

citations.title: Bending the power law
citations.quote: Estimated decay rates for short-session RT speed-ups clustered near 0.25-0.45 in healthy adults.

practice_effect.rationale: Dual-task and Sternberg paradigms show pronounced practice speed-up during early blocks (~50-100ms offset) reaching asymptote by ~block 3 (Logan 1988 instance theory; Rickard 1997). The integrated memory + stop-signal design demands strategy consolidation, supporting an early-session offset.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrement effects emerge primarily in continuous-performance / monotonous monitoring tasks; the integrated stop-signal+memory paradigm involves active probe-by-probe engagement and is not documented to show classic vigilance-decrement increases in RT variability within typical session lengths.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Healthy-adult between-subject RT mean SDs in stop-signal and Sternberg tasks typically run ~50-70ms; condition-specific SDs ~25-35ms (Matzke et al. 2013; Bissett & Logan 2011). Accuracy SD ~3-5% reflects ceiling-bounded variability on simple shape discrimination and Sternberg probe accuracy in healthy adults. Standard clip ranges retained: this paradigm has no atypical performance regime (not perceptual-threshold, not slow-paced) requiring overrides.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean go-RT in healthy adult samples ranged from 45 to 75 ms across cohorts.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean go-RT was approximately 50-65 ms in young-adult samples.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs for condition-specific mean RTs in Sternberg tasks were typically 25-40 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS
citations.quote: Within-condition between-subject SD of mean RT was about 25-35 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (between-subject ranges of sigma and tau)
citations.quote: Between-subject sigma and tau estimates spanned roughly 0.8x to 1.2x the sample mean for healthy adult cohorts.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Individual sigma and tau parameters varied within roughly 85-115% of the group mean.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of go-trial accuracy was 0.03-0.05 in healthy young adults at near-ceiling performance.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Sternberg probe accuracy ranged from .92 to .99 across subjects; between-subject SD ~0.03-0.04.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands
citations.quote: Go-trial omission (no-response) rates ranged from <0.01 to about 0.04 across subjects; SD ~0.01.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS
citations.quote: Healthy-adult go-omission rates were typically below 5%, with between-subject SD around 1-2%.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands
citations.quote: Subjects with go-accuracy below approximately 0.60 were excluded from analyses; accuracy in retained subjects ranged from ~0.65 to 1.00.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions in the study of cognitive processes
citations.quote: Probe accuracy ranged from .60 (low-end exclusion threshold) up to 1.00 across the analyzed sample.

citations.authors: Matzke, D.; Love, J.; Wiecki, T.V.; Brown, S.D.; Logan, G.D.; Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Release the BEESTS
citations.quote: Subjects with go-omission rates above approximately 10% were excluded; retained subjects had omission rates from 0 to about 0.10.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands
citations.quote: Go-omission rates above ~0.10 led to exclusion; analyzed sample omission rates fell between 0.00 and 0.10.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_with_integrated_memory' with 5 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-. Applied expfactory platform-default nav phases (0 → 10).
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 4 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects.
Produced 50 citations across 26 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 33 parameters; 7 are high-sensitivity.
